title: Executive Order on the Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence
official_title: Executive Order 14110 — Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence
overview: Executive Order 14110 is the primary US federal AI policy instrument. It directs federal agencies to take specific actions to manage AI risks, promote AI safety standards, protect privacy, advance equity, and support American AI leadership. While EOs do not bind private companies directly, they establish reporting requirements for dual-use foundation model developers, direct NIST to develop new standards, and require federal agencies that procure or deploy AI to follow specific practices. Many provisions create regulatory precursors that are likely to become binding rules.

key_directives.title: Safety and security of AI
key_directives.description: Developers of dual-use foundation models above certain compute thresholds must report training runs and red-team results to the federal government under the Defense Production Act.
key_directives.threshold: Models trained using a quantity of computing power greater than 10^26 integer or floating-point operations
key_directives.applies_to: Developers of dual-use foundation AI models
key_directives.deadline: Ongoing — reportable as training runs are initiated

key_directives.title: Standards for AI safety
key_directives.description: NIST directed to establish AI safety and security standards and testing procedures. AISI (AI Safety Institute) established within NIST to develop guidelines, tools, and best practices.
key_directives.applies_to: Federal agencies; standards influence private sector practice

key_directives.title: Protecting American privacy
key_directives.description: Federal agencies directed to use privacy-enhancing technologies and evaluate how commercial data brokers supply data for AI training. Calls for federal legislation on data privacy.
key_directives.applies_to: Federal agencies; informs private sector expectations

key_directives.title: Algorithmic discrimination protections
key_directives.description: Federal agencies directed to use existing civil rights authorities to protect the public from algorithmic discrimination. Agencies must issue guidance on AI and civil rights in their domains.
key_directives.applies_to: Federal agencies and AI systems used in federally-regulated sectors

key_directives.title: Supporting workers
key_directives.description: Agencies directed to develop principles and best practices for mitigating AI harms to workers, including displacement, surveillance, and erosion of worker voice.
key_directives.applies_to: Federal contractors; influences employer obligations under existing labor law

key_directives.title: Federal agency AI use
key_directives.description: OMB directed to issue guidance on federal agency AI use and procurement. Agencies must designate a Chief AI Officer. Federal AI use governed by principles of safety, security, privacy, equity, and civil rights.
key_directives.applies_to: All federal agencies and federal contractors

key_directives.title: AI content authentication
key_directives.description: Commerce Department to develop guidance on content authentication and watermarking of AI-generated content. Federal agencies directed to use tools to authenticate AI-generated content.
key_directives.applies_to: Federal agencies; creates market expectation for content watermarking

private_sector_implications: Large foundation model developers above the compute threshold must report training runs to the government
Companies providing AI to federal agencies must comply with OMB AI procurement guidance
AI systems used in hiring, credit, housing, or healthcare face heightened civil rights scrutiny from federal regulators
Content generated by AI systems used in federal contexts should be authenticated or watermarked
Companies should anticipate that NIST standards referenced in EO 14110 will be incorporated into future binding regulations

subsequent_actions.action: OMB M-24-10: Advancing Governance, Innovation, and Risk Management for Agency Use of AI
subsequent_actions.significance: Binding on federal agencies; establishes AI governance requirements for federal procurement
Guidance on managing risks specific to generative AI; referenced in federal procurement

subsequent_actions.action: Commerce Department dual-use foundation model reporting rule
subsequent_actions.significance: Proposed rulemaking to implement Section 4 reporting requirements

cross_references.relevance: EO 14110 elevates the NIST AI RMF to de facto federal standard; companies selling to the US government should treat RMF as quasi-mandatory
AI provisions are being incorporated into FAR for federal procurement; contractors will face binding AI requirements
